title: The World Is Watching People Move. Your Turn.
trend: World Cup 2026 round-of-16 week; theme-only response using original copy and art, with no teams, players, fixtures, logos, scorelines, or footage.
hook: A small proposal for the millions currently yelling 'MAKE THE RUN' from a sofa.

slides: A small proposal for everyone yelling ‘MAKE THE RUN’ from a sofa.
You have correctly identified that movement creates possibility.
Beautiful analysis. No notes.
Now apply it locally: one court, one route, one hour, actual humans.
Watch the knockout. Then make a move that isn't tactical commentary.
Meet through movement. KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access in bio.
body.caption: The world is watching people move, improvise, recover, and show up for one another. Excellent. Now borrow ten percent of that energy for your own city. Watch the knockout, then organize something real. KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access in bio.
body.cta: Send this to the tactical genius in your group chat and pick a sport.
body.imageConcept: Six original 4:5 typographic cards. A neon-green tactical arrow begins trapped inside a hand-drawn sofa outline, breaks free by card four, and becomes a route toward a generic court. No tournament marks, flags, kits, fixtures, people, or broadcast imagery. Alt text: an illustrated green movement arrow travels from a sofa to a local sports court.

title: Penalty Shootout Energy, Applied To The Group Chat
trend: World Cup knockout-stage suspense translated into an original group-chat joke; no match-specific reference.
hook: Five people. Five messages. One of you has to actually choose a time.

body.caption: The group chat has entered penalties. ‘I’m easy’ is not a time. ‘Whatever works’ is not a venue. Step up, pick the court, save the friendship. KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access in bio.
body.cta: Tag the person who always takes the deciding message.
body.imageConcept: Single 4:5 mock scoreboard made entirely from original shapes. Five chat bubbles replace penalty dots: four muted bubbles read 'whatever works'; the fifth glows neon green and reads 'Tuesday, 19:00 — booked.' Tiny KeepItUp mark at bottom. Alt text: a playful scoreboard of vague group-chat replies ending with one concrete sports plan.

title: Your Friend During The Knockouts vs On The Court
trend: World Cup tactical-commentary culture adapted into an original split-character comedy reel without event footage or audio.
hook: POV: your friend has elite spatial awareness until you ask them to move three metres left.

body.script:
0:00–0:02 — Original text card: ‘YOUR FRIEND WATCHING THE KNOCKOUTS.’ Faceless hands move bottle caps around a blank tabletop like a tactical board. Voice: ‘The half-space is completely open.’
0:02–0:05 — More intense pointing. ‘They need to rotate earlier and attack the channel.’
0:05–0:07 — Hard cut to a generic padel/tennis court made from simple animation. Text: ‘THE SAME FRIEND PLAYING.’
0:07–0:10 — A ball rolls slowly between two illustrated feet. Voice: ‘Was that mine?’
0:10–0:13 — Freeze frame: ‘Perfect. Invite them anyway.’
0:13–0:16 — End card: ‘Experts optional. Showing up required. KeepItUp — open worldwide.’ Use original percussion only; no broadcast sound or event branding.
body.caption: Elite analysis. Developing lateral movement. Still invited. KeepItUp helps adults organize small sports meetups worldwide — early access in bio.
body.cta: Send this to your most tactically gifted, physically stationary friend.
body.imageConcept: 9:16 cover: bottle-cap tactical arrows above, one gently missed ball below, separated by a neon-green line. Original art only. Alt text: a humorous split screen contrasts confident sports analysis with missing an easy ball.

title: Wimbledon Green Flags You Can Actually Observe
trend: Wimbledon 2026 fortnight; rides tennis conversation through universal court etiquette rather than players, results, protected branding, or imagery.
hook: A polished bio can say anything. A close rally has witnesses.

slides: Wimbledon week, but make the green flags useful.
Calls their own line honestly — even when the point matters.
Adjusts the pace when you're learning. Doesn't turn your first game into an audition.
Returns the stray ball. Thanks the person at the desk. Notices who else is there.
Can lose a point without making the atmosphere lose with it.
A polished bio can say anything. A close rally has witnesses.
Meet through movement. KeepItUp — open worldwide, early access in bio.
body.caption: Tennis reveals the tiny things: honesty, patience, how someone handles a bad bounce, whether the game stays fun when they stop winning. Those are much more interesting than ‘fluent in sarcasm.’ KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access in bio.
body.cta: Save this for your next game, or comment the court green flag we missed.
body.imageConcept: Seven 4:5 cards with an original neon-green line-call motif: honest IN/OUT marks, a rolling ball, and subtle rally arcs. No grass-court photography, Wimbledon wordmark, trophy, athletes, uniforms, or broadcast visual language. Alt text: illustrated tennis etiquette cards listing observable green flags during a friendly match.

title: Love Means Zero. Effort Does Not.
trend: Wimbledon fortnight and the globally understood tennis-score meaning of love; original wordplay rather than event-specific commentary.
hook: LOVE: 0. EFFORT: visible from the baseline.

body.caption: Tennis is the only place where love can mean zero and effort still tells you everything. Who chases the awkward ball? Who laughs off the frame shot? Who checks your pace? Behavior over bio. KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access in bio.
body.cta: Share this with the person who never lets the awkward ball die.
body.imageConcept: Single 4:5 minimalist scorecard: LOVE 0 in off-white; EFFORT ∞ in neon green. One imperfect hand-drawn ball arc escapes the scoreboard and continues off-card. Original typography, no event marks. Alt text: a minimalist tennis scorecard contrasts love at zero with effort at infinity.

title: The Tour de Green Flags
trend: Tour de France 2026 opening weekend in Barcelona; riffs on teamwork, pacing, and support without riders, teams, jerseys, route maps, results, or race branding.
hook: The hottest person on the climb is not always the one in front.

slides: The hottest person on the climb is not always the one in front.
Sometimes it's the one who notices you're dropping back.
Matches your pace without announcing that they're matching your pace.
Points out the pothole. Shares the water. Waits at the turn.
Makes the hard bit feel shared — not graded.
The Tour has leaders. A good first ride has teammates.
Meet through movement. KeepItUp — open worldwide, early access in bio.
body.caption: The Tour starts with speed. We’re more interested in pace: the person who notices, adjusts, points out the rough patch, and makes the hard bit feel shared. That kind of chemistry is difficult to write in a bio and very easy to spot on a ride. Open worldwide — early access in bio.
body.cta: Tag the rider who waits at the top without making it weird.
body.imageConcept: Seven original 4:5 cards. Two abstract wheel-lines begin at different heights, synchronize by slide three, curve around a pothole, and finish side by side. Anthracite, neon green, off-white. No yellow jersey, race logo, official map, teams, riders, or Barcelona landmarks. Alt text: two illustrated cycling lines gradually match pace and finish together.

title: The Pace-Match Plot Twist
trend: Tour de France opening-week conversation adapted into a short original cinematic reveal about compatibility through pace.
hook: Plot twist: they weren't slow. They were staying with you.

body.script:
0:00–0:03 — Two abstract bicycle-wheel shadows on pavement, one repeatedly easing back. Text: ‘I thought they were having a bad ride.’
0:03–0:06 — The second shadow catches up; both settle into the same rhythm. Text changes: ‘They were matching my pace.’
0:06–0:09 — Small illustrated hand signal points around a pothole. Original soft wheel-click audio.
0:09–0:12 — Both stop at a generic overlook; no faces or identifiable place. Text: ‘Chemistry sometimes looks like not getting dropped.’
0:12–0:15 — End card: ‘Behavior over bio. Meet through movement. KeepItUp — open worldwide.’ No race footage, jerseys, logos, route, or trending commercial audio.
body.caption: Plot twist: compatibility can look like somebody quietly choosing the same pace. Behavior over bio. KeepItUp is open worldwide — early access in bio.
body.cta: Send this to the person who never leaves the group behind.
body.imageConcept: 9:16 cover with two abstract wheel shadows converging into one neon-green rhythm line. Text: ‘THEY WERE MATCHING YOUR PACE.’ Original, faceless, location-neutral art. Alt text: two cycling shadows converge as one rider slows to match the other's pace.

title: Watch One, Play One — Global Sports Weekend Poll
trend: World Cup knockouts, Wimbledon, and the Tour de France overlapping in one global sports weekend; interactive participation prompt.
hook: You can watch all three. But which one would you actually play tomorrow?

slides: GLOBAL SPORTS WEEKEND: WATCH ONE. PLAY ONE.
After the football: five-a-side or a walk with a ball? [POLL: PLAY / RECOVER]
After the tennis: rally or doubles? [POLL: RALLY / DOUBLES]
After the cycling: café loop or hill attempt? [POLL: CAFÉ / CLIMB]
Watching counts as joy. Playing creates a story. Which one are you organizing?
body.caption: A low-pressure participation poll for the weekend when football, tennis, and cycling all own the timeline.
body.cta: Use the native poll on each frame; final-frame link sticker points to early access.
body.imageConcept: Five 9:16 story frames using three original motion glyphs: a rolling circle, a rally arc, and two wheel loops. Clear poll-safe empty zones and high-contrast text. No event logos, footage, athletes, or official iconography. Alt text: interactive story polls ask viewers which football, tennis, or cycling activity they would play.
